FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD study (4.0 release), baseline 2016-2018, 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying and traditional bullying victimization only. No qualifying smartphone/SM exposure. B3 applies: cyberbullying alone NOT_MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models examining ACEs/bullying → internalizing/externalizing outcomes with coefficients reported (Table 2).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients with 95% CI reported in Table 2 for main effects and interaction models.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD data with regression models predicting mental health outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; appears as original research article.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT-IC2 (no qualifying SM/smartphone exposure), so FT-EC7 does not apply. Additionally, paper examines ACEs/bullying → mental health (not SM as DV), and predictors include ACEs which is excluded per B17.
notes: Cyberbullying is mentioned in abstract/methods but treated as part of bullying victimization construct only. No separate SM exposure measure. Paper excluded at FT-IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD study (data release 5.0), baseline ages 9-10 years, 4-year annual follow-up (T0-T4)

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (hours/day): self-reported via Youth Screen Time Survey, categorized as social media (items 4+5+6: social networking sites, texting, video chat). Qualifying SM per B1 with separate coefficients.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed models with 2-way (DM use × time) and 3-way (DM use × time × PGS-ADHD) interactions testing longitudinal associations with ADHD inattention symptoms; β coefficients reported (Table 2).

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized β (SE) with Bonferroni-corrected p-values reported in Table 2 (main); cumulative effect β=0.15 (SE 0.03) T4-T0 reported in text and Figure 2B.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD cohort with longitudinal regression models predicting ADHD-related symptom change over 4 years.

FT_IC6.evidence: No evidence of duplicate; published 2026 in Pediatrics Open Science.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper does NOT meet FT-EC7 criteria because: (a) SM is NOT the dependent variable in all analyses — SM is the independent variable predicting ADHD symptoms (outcome is inattention/hyperactivity-impulsivity); (b) Model includes non-demographic predictors: PGS-ADHD (polygenic risk score = genetic predictor, excluded per B17); age at T0, sex, SES, study sites (covariates); (c) longitudinal design present but condition (a) fails immediately. Per B18 carve-out: has SM-as-IV → outcome analysis so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Strong candidate for inclusion. Social media measured as separate SM variable with own coefficient. Gaming and TV/video use also measured separately. Longitudinal with strong directionality evidence (cross-lagged models; reverse association negligible). Robust across sensitivity analyses.

FT_IC1.evidence: Title mentions ABCD study but no PDF available to confirm full dataset details, sample size, or release version.

FT_IC2.evidence: Abstract mentions 'screen time' but lacks detail. Unable to determine from abstract alone whether screen time includes qualifying SM/smartphone variables (platform-specific, SM accounts, texting, video chat) or is unspecified 'screen time' (B5 NOT_MET category). Requires full PDF.

FT_IC3.evidence: Abstract mentions screen time in context of depression outcomes but no test type or citation provided without full text.

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; cannot extract numeric data from tables or supplement.

FT_IC5.evidence: Title and abstract indicate ABCD analysis but full study design cannot be verified without PDF.

FT_IC6.evidence: No PDF to assess for duplicate status.

FT_EC7_reason: Cannot determine EC7 status without full PDF content. Presumed NOT_APPLY pending review.
notes: NO PDF AVAILABLE for paper 303. Classification cannot be finalized without full-text access. Recommend obtaining PDF and re-screening. Abstract reference to 'screen time' is insufficient to determine if it qualifies (B5 exclusion for unspecified screen time likely if not SM-specific).
